common_name: New Gold Lantana (Sterile Golden-Yellow Pollinator Lantana for Gulf South)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; New Gold is sterile and will not produce the potentially invasive berries of the straight species — an important environmental advantage for Gulf South Gulf Coast plantings near natural areas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; New Gold is a sterile hybrid lantana producing a continuous flush of bright golden-yellow to deep-gold flowers from spring through frost — one of the most floriferous and pollinator-attractive annuals and perennials for Gulf South full-sun gardens. The sterile nature of 'New Gold' is its most important horticultural virtue: unlike Lantana camara (common lantana), which is invasive in coastal areas of Florida and Texas where its berries are consumed and dispersed by birds, 'New Gold' produces no viable seed and no berries, eliminating the invasive spread concern that limits use of the species in sensitive native habitats. The golden-yellow flower color provides a particularly effective nectar resource for monarch and queen butterflies, swallowtails, skippers, and numerous native bee species — the warm-yellow flowers are among the most reliably butterfly-visited of any commonly grown annual in the Gulf South. In zone 8, New Gold is a perennial returning from the root each spring after winter dieback; in zone 10-11, it may persist as an evergreen subshrub. The trailing to mounding habit makes it outstanding for sunny borders, retaining wall edges, and large container plantings where the branches cascade naturally. Extremely drought-tolerant once established; excess water and nitrogen reduce flowering.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting, or direct sow after last frost in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fireworks Gomphrena is among the most heat-tolerant summer annuals — it thrives in the brutal Gulf South August heat that collapses most other summer annuals.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Fireworks Gomphrena is an All-America Selections award-winning annual producing one of the most distinctive and arresting flower forms available in a summer annual — elongated, branching, multi-headed inflorescences with deep magenta-pink to fuchsia base flowers tipped with bright golden-yellow, creating a visual resemblance to exploding fireworks that gives the variety its name. Unlike the compact, ball-shaped flower heads of standard Gomphrena globosa, 'Fireworks' produces taller, more branched stems with elongated floral structures that create an airy, open texture radically different from the round button flowers of globe amaranth. The plants bloom from summer through frost with no deadheading required — the spent flower heads dry naturally on the plant, providing additional ornamental interest and wildlife seed value in the fall garden. Fireworks Gomphrena is one of the most reliable Gulf South summer annuals for sustained performance in brutal heat: where impatiens, petunias, and other summer annuals collapse in July-August humidity and heat, Fireworks continues to bloom without interruption. Butterfly gardens benefit greatly from this variety — monarch butterflies, skippers, and gulf fritillaries visit the flowers prolifically. The upright, open habit works well as a background annual in mixed beds and as a cut flower with exceptional vase life.

common_name: Harbour Dwarf Nandina (Low-Growing Spreading Dwarf Heavenly Bamboo)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Harbour Dwarf spreads slowly by underground stolons to form a widening mat — a useful quality for groundcover purposes but give it room to spread or plan to manage the spread.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Harbour Dwarf is a distinctly low, spreading dwarf nandina (heavenly bamboo) selection reaching only 18–24 inches in height while spreading steadily wider by underground stolons — creating a slowly spreading, bamboo-textured groundcover mass with fine-textured, compound, pinnate foliage that turns shades of red, orange, and bronze in fall and winter. The fine, ferny texture of nandina foliage is unique among commonly grown shrubs — the small, lance-shaped leaflets create an almost grass-like visual lightness despite the woody, evergreen character of the plant. Unlike the large, upright nandina forms (which can reach 6–8 feet), Harbour Dwarf remains permanently low, making it appropriate for the front of foundation plantings, as a border edging, and as a shade-tolerant groundcover alternative to liriope or mondo grass. Fall and winter bring progressively more vivid red-orange coloration that intensifies after frost — some of the most reliable fall foliage color available in a compact evergreen shrub for Gulf South gardens. The spreading habit and shade tolerance make Harbour Dwarf one of the few ornamental woody plants useful as a groundcover under trees and in partially shaded borders. Extremely adaptable: tolerates drought, clay soils, and part shade once established.

common_name: Firepower Nandina (Compact Brilliant-Red Winter-Color Heavenly Bamboo)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Firepower's most dramatic color display occurs in full sun with cold winter temperatures — the combination of sun exposure and cold triggers the most vivid scarlet-red foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Firepower is a compact, mounded dwarf nandina selection producing some of the most vivid fall and winter foliage color available in any compact evergreen shrub — the fine-textured, compound foliage transitions from lime-green in summer through orange to brilliant scarlet-red in fall and winter, with the most intense red occurring during and after the coldest periods. The bright red winter color of 'Firepower' is significantly more intense than the reddish tones of most other nandina cultivars, making it particularly outstanding in winter landscapes when few other evergreen shrubs provide warm color. In Gulf South winters (mild in zone 9), the red coloration is variable — coldest winters produce the best red; mild winters produce more orange and red-orange. The compact, naturally rounded habit (2–3 feet) requires minimal pruning and maintains its shape without shearing, making Firepower one of the most truly low-maintenance landscape shrubs for Gulf South foundation plantings, parking lot islands, and mass plantings. The fine bamboo-textured foliage creates an appealing contrast with bold-leaved plants. 'Firepower' does not produce berries (it is functionally sterile), eliminating the concern about nandina berry toxicity to birds that has arisen in some regions.

common_name: Butterfly Deep Red Pentas (Heat-Proof Red Star-Cluster Hummingbird Plant)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pentas is one of the few red-flowering annuals that reliably attracts hummingbirds — the tubular, star-shaped flower clusters are perfectly sized for hummingbird feeding and are visited throughout the bloom season.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Butterfly Deep Red is a Pentas lanceolata cultivar in the Butterfly series — producing dense, rounded clusters of tubular, star-shaped flowers in deep, rich red throughout the warm season. Pentas (star cluster) is one of the most valuable warm-season annuals for Gulf South butterfly and hummingbird gardens: the flower clusters are a concentrated nectar resource attracting monarch butterflies, gulf fritillaries, zebra longwings, sulphurs, hummingbirds, and numerous other pollinators in a steady stream through the long Gulf South summer. The deep red of 'Butterfly Deep Red' is particularly effective at attracting hummingbirds, which strongly prefer red tubular flowers. Pentas blooms without pause from planting through frost in zone 9, maintaining vigorous flower production through the brutal Gulf South August heat that reduces or stops flowering in many other summer annuals — one of the few ornamentals that looks better in August than in June. The Butterfly series was bred specifically for compact habit, continuous bloom, and heat tolerance, making it more reliable in Gulf South conditions than older, taller pentas varieties. In zone 10-12, Pentas grows as a perennial subshrub; in zone 9, it is a warm-season annual that may self-sow in sheltered, mild winters. Plant in masses for maximum visual impact and to create a substantial nectar resource for migrating monarchs in late summer and fall.
